cards.front: What two function properties are used to differentiate overloaded functions?
cards.back:
• Number of parameters
  • Type of parameters (excluding typedefs, type aliases, and const on value parameters)

cards.front: Is the return type used to differentiate overloaded functions?
cards.back: No. The return type is not used for function overload differentiation.

cards.question: Are these two functions properly differentiated?
cards.code:
int add(int x, int y);
int add(int x, int y, int z);
cards.answer: Yes, they are differentiated by the number of parameters (2 vs 3).
cards.explanation: Functions with different numbers of parameters are considered distinct overloads.

cards.question: Will this code compile?
cards.explanation: Type aliases and typedefs are not distinct types. All three functions have the same signature: void print(int), so they are not differentiated.

cards.text: For parameters passed by value, the {{c1::const}} qualifier is not considered for differentiation.

cards.question: Are these functions differentiated?
cards.explanation: For pass-by-value parameters, const is ignored for overload differentiation. Both functions have the same signature.

cards.question: Are these functions properly differentiated?
cards.answer: Yes, all four functions are properly differentiated.
cards.explanation: Each function has a distinct list of parameter types, so they are all valid overloads.

cards.text: For parameters passed by {{c1::(const) reference}} or {{c2::pointer}}, the const qualifier is considered for differentiation.

cards.question: Are these functions differentiated?
cards.code:
void print(int& x);
void print(const int& x);
cards.explanation: For reference parameters, const is considered for differentiation. These are two different overloads.

cards.front: Can you overload a function solely based on different return types?
cards.back: No. Return types are not used for overload differentiation. The functions must differ in number or type of parameters.

cards.concept: Why return type is not used for overload differentiation
cards.explanation: Return type is not used because function calls can appear in contexts where the return type is ignored (e.g., void context). The compiler wouldn't be able to determine which overload to call based solely on the return type.
examples: getValue();  // Which one? Can't tell from return type!

cards.text: An overloaded function is differentiated so long as each function has a different {{c1::number of parameters}} or {{c2::list of parameter types}}.
